Q1: I organized my study routine around visual learning primarily - I'd set aside blocks of time to watch lecture videos and create diagrams or mind maps from the content. With 19 online courses to juggle, I couldn't attend every live session, so I relied heavily on recorded materials. I typically studied in the evenings after my other responsibilities, spending around 12 hours a week focused on coursework. My routine was about efficiency rather than consistency, fitting study time wherever I could manage it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, but honestly, deadlines drove a lot of my schedule. Being 24 and having various commitments outside of academics, I had to be flexible. When assignments were approaching, I'd ramp up significantly, but during quieter periods, I might spread things out more evenly across the week.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download materials for upcoming work. Sometimes I'd get notifications about new content being posted, and that would prompt me to log in. I also checked in when I wanted to see feedback on submitted work or needed to participate in discussions for marks. It wasn't really a daily habit - more purpose-driven when I had specific tasks to complete.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never quite became a natural part of my routine the way some students describe. I used it when necessary, but I preferred downloading materials and working offline. The times it felt most routine were during heavy assignment periods when I was constantly checking rubrics and submission portals. But even then, it felt more like a tool I visited rather than a space I inhabited.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable for me as a visual learner - I watched nearly 200 minutes of content because seeing concepts explained visually really helped things click. I also found the assignment submission areas useful because the rubrics and guidelines were clear. The discussion boards were helpful occasionally when peers shared insights or resources I hadn't considered. Anything that let me see information presented graphically or in video format worked best for my learning style.
Q6: I often ignored the reading-heavy resources or lengthy text documents, preferring to find visual alternatives or summaries. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant if I'd already grasped the concept from videos. I also minimally used the live chat features because they didn't fit my schedule well. If something required real-time participation and I couldn't make it, I'd just skip it and find another way to learn the material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when it directly connected to assessment outcomes or genuinely deepened my understanding. If I could see how something would help me with an assignment or exam, I was motivated to engage with it. Visual content that explained complex ideas efficiently always felt valuable. I also appreciated activities where I could see immediate progress or application of what I was learning.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different - I approached them with more skepticism about whether they'd be worth the time investment. Required activities I'd complete regardless of my interest level because they affected my grade. With optional ones, I'd evaluate whether they'd actually help me learn or just add to my workload. Given my medium resources and busy schedule, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture videos and PDFs, then work through them on my own time without being connected. I created handwritten notes and visual diagrams that I'd study from repeatedly. I also used external YouTube videos and other resources to supplement course materials when I needed different explanations. My actual learning time far exceeded what any login data would show.
Q10: There were many situations where I'd be reviewing my printed notes or hand-drawn diagrams for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sometimes rewatch downloaded videos multiple times while taking notes, but that wouldn't register as additional views. Group study sessions with peers where we discussed course content happened completely outside the system. My most intensive review periods before exams were almost entirely offline.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I needed, and then study them offline extensively. This was especially true for video content - I'd download lectures and watch them multiple times at my own pace. It saved me from relying on internet connectivity and let me study anywhere. The initial access was just the starting point for much deeper offline engagement.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when multiple deadlines converged across my 19 courses. My attendance was quite low at around 15%, partly because competing demands made it difficult to be present for everything. The moderate anxiety I experienced sometimes made it harder to start tasks even when I knew they were important. When stress peaked, I'd focus on survival mode - just getting essential assignments done rather than engaging deeply.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a real factor - at 24, I have responsibilities beyond just being a student, and sometimes I'd log into the LMS exhausted and just not absorb anything. Confusion about course requirements occasionally led me to avoid certain sections until I could clarify things. When workload from multiple courses piled up, I'd prioritize based on deadlines and point values, which meant some LMS activities got minimal attention.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings but my mind was elsewhere - stressed about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd technically be 'watching' content but not really processing it, which meant I'd have to revisit it later. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really engaging with the ideas being shared. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they might have registered as activity.
Q15: I postponed discussion board participation quite a bit, even though I knew it mattered. The moderate anxiety I deal with made it feel intimidating to put my thoughts out publicly. I'd also delay starting larger assignments because the initial overwhelm was hard to push through. Eventually my resilience would kick in and I'd power through, but there was often avoidance at the beginning of challenging tasks.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my primary driver for LMS usage. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached, making sure I understood requirements and had submitted everything correctly. My assignment completion rate was high because I took deadlines seriously, even if my engagement was concentrated in bursts rather than spread evenly. The structure of deadlines helped me prioritize across my many courses.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement - when I received constructive comments, I'd often revisit course materials to understand where I could improve. Positive feedback motivated me to maintain my approach, while critical feedback sometimes surprised me and prompted reflection. I appreciated when feedback came quickly because it helped me adjust for subsequent assignments. The feedback loop was important for my sense of progress.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation significantly. When materials were organized logically and visually accessible, I engaged more readily. The flexibility to access recorded content suited my irregular schedule better than mandatory live sessions. However, when the structure was unclear or navigation was confusing, I'd spend less time exploring and just focus on essentials. Clear pathways through content helped me stay on track.
Q19: Visual organization of the LMS made engagement easier - when I could see the course layout clearly and understand what was expected, I felt more confident navigating it. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy resources without visual alternatives. The requirement for real-time participation in some activities was difficult given my schedule. Having downloadable content was crucial for my learning style and situation.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding without finding it - clicking through materials trying to make sense of things but not producing work because they're still confused. They might also be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism that makes them engage with content repeatedly but struggle to finalize submissions. Sometimes high activity reflects genuine effort that doesn't translate into completed work due to external circumstances or overwhelm.
Q21: This actually reflects my experience quite well. My LMS engagement level was technically low, but I performed at Merit level because so much of my learning happened offline. I downloaded materials, created my own study resources, and engaged deeply with content in ways the system couldn't track. Students who are efficient and self-directed might not need constant LMS interaction to succeed. Quality of engagement matters more than quantity of logins.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access timing and which specific resources students click on. They capture submission behaviors and basic participation metrics. However, they miss the depth of offline engagement, the quality of attention during access, and the learning that happens through external resources or peer discussions. They can't measure understanding, only activity. The difference between clicking and comprehending is invisible to the system.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and trying to understand it deeply, not just completing tasks mechanically. For me, it involves creating visual representations of concepts and connecting ideas across different sources. It's about caring enough to seek understanding even when it's difficult. True engagement is internal - it's curiosity and effort applied to learning, regardless of what any tracking system might record.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show the hours I spent creating hand-drawn diagrams and mind maps to process information visually. It wouldn't capture my study sessions with peers where we discussed and debated course concepts. My use of external resources to supplement understanding is invisible. Most importantly, it can't show my resilience - how I pushed through anxiety and stress to complete assignments at a high level despite the challenges I faced. The internal struggle and eventual triumph don't leave digital footprints.
